questions.question: What is the primary advantage of multicast over unicast for video distribution?
options: Multicast sends one copy that forks at routers, reducing bandwidth vs. N unicast copies
questions.explanation: Multicast sends a single copy of each packet that is duplicated only at branch points in the distribution tree, saving bandwidth compared to unicast which sends N separate copies.

questions.question: What is the role of the Rendezvous Point (RP) in PIM-SM?
options: It is the root of the shared tree where sources register and receivers join
questions.explanation: The RP is the central meeting point: sources register their streams with the RP, and receivers join the shared tree rooted at the RP to receive traffic.

questions.question: What triggers the SPT (Shortest Path Tree) switchover in PIM-SM?
options: When traffic on the shared tree exceeds a configured rate threshold, the DR joins directly toward the source
questions.explanation: When a receiver's DR detects that traffic on the shared tree exceeds the SPT threshold, it sends a PIM Join directly toward the source, creating a shortest-path tree and then pruning the shared tree.

questions.question: What happens when the last receiver on a LAN leaves a multicast group?
options: The DR sends a PIM Prune toward the RP, removing that branch from the tree
questions.explanation: When no receivers remain on a LAN, the DR sends a PIM Prune message toward the RP, which prunes that branch from the distribution tree and stops traffic flowing to that LAN.

questions.question: What is the difference between the shared tree (*,G) and the source tree (S,G)?
options: They are the same thing with different names
The shared tree is rooted at the RP for all sources; the source tree is rooted at a specific source for lower latency
The source tree uses TCP; the shared tree uses UDP
The shared tree is always faster
questions.explanation: The shared tree (*,G) is rooted at the RP and carries traffic from all sources for group G. The source tree (S,G) is rooted at a specific source S, providing a shorter path to receivers after switchover.
